title: Candied citrus peel
subtitle: Orange and lemon peel blanched three times then candied in sugar syrup
excerpt: Thick strips of orange and lemon peel blanched three times to remove bitterness, then simmered slowly in a heavy sugar syrup until translucent and tender. The peel is lifted out, drained on a rack and left to dry before being rolled in granulated sugar or dipped in dark chocolate. The multiple blanching rounds are the step that separates pleasantly bittersweet candied peel from harsh, chewy peel that is difficult to eat. The total active time is about 1 hour; drying takes several hours more.
sourceNotes: Candied citrus peel appears in confectionery manuals from at least the seventeenth century as a method of preserving fruit peel. Elizabeth David includes it in 'Italian Food' (1954) and Delia Smith gives a classic British version in 'Delia's Complete Cookery Course' (1982). The triple-blanching method is documented in French patisserie texts including Gaston Lenotre's 'Lenotre's Desserts and Pastries' (1977).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fully dried, sugared candied peel keeps in an airtight jar at room temperature for up to 3 weeks. Do not refrigerate as moisture will cause it to clump.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; all cooking done on the stovetop

glossaryTerms.definition: Bringing peel to the boil in fresh cold water, draining, and repeating with fresh water two more times. Citrus peel contains naringin and limonin, bitter compounds concentrated in the white pith. Blanching extracts these compounds progressively into the boiling water: the first blanch removes most of the bitterness, the second removes more, and the third leaves a mild, pleasantly bittersweet flavour that works well in a sweet application. Skipping blanches produces peel that is too bitter for most people to eat as a sweet. The number of blanches can be adjusted: two produce more bitterness, four almost none. Three is the standard for general-purpose candied peel.

content.text: The step most often skipped, and the one that makes the most difference, is

. Each blanch in fresh cold water drawn to the boil removes another layer of bitterness from the pith. Skip to one blanch and the peel will be too bitter; skip all three and it will be close to inedible.

Use unwaxed fruit. Waxed citrus skin has a coating that resists the sugar syrup and produces peel that looks dull rather than translucent. If only waxed fruit is available, scrub well under hot water with a stiff brush before peeling.

items.prepNote: peel only, fruit reserved for another use
peel only, fruit reserved for another use

content.text: Score the peel of each fruit into quarters and peel away, keeping as much white pith as possible attached. Cut each piece into strips about 5 mm wide. Place in a medium saucepan, cover with cold water and bring to the boil. Drain, return to the pan, cover again with fresh cold water and bring back to the boil. Drain. Repeat once more with fresh cold water; this is the third blanch. Drain and set aside.

Combine the 400 g granulated sugar and 300 ml water in the saucepan. Bring to the boil, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Add the blanched peel strips and reduce to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the peel is translucent, tender and the syrup has thickened. The peel is ready when it looks glassy and the syrup coats a spoon. Do not cook until the syrup crystalises.

Lift the strips out with a slotted spoon and arrange in a single layer on a wire rack set over a baking tray. Leave to dry at room temperature for at least 4 hours, or overnight, until the surface is tacky but not wet. Roll in granulated sugar to coat, or leave plain. Store in an airtight jar.

items.problem: Peel is still too bitter to eat pleasantly
items.solution: Only one or two blanches were done. The third blanch removes the last layer of bitterness. If it is still too bitter after three, add a fourth blanch with fresh water.

items.problem: Syrup crystallised in the pan before the peel was fully cooked
items.solution: The heat was too high or the pan was disturbed. Reduce to a gentle simmer and do not stir excessively. If crystallisation starts, add a splash of water and heat gently to dissolve.

items.problem: Peel is sticky and never dried firm enough to handle
items.solution: It needs more drying time. Leave on the rack overnight in a cool, dry place. A low oven at 50 degrees C with the door ajar can speed this up if humidity is high.
